metadata.title: The Engineer Who Fixes What Nobody Asked Him To
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Sayam Khatri
tags: The Engineer Who Fixes What Nobody Asked
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Sayam Khatri — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Building things nobody requested, like his A/B testing frameworks, is a different trait from ambition. The work he took on was explicitly not in scope and unassigned to him. For three years in Haryana he operated inside the H.A.S.P. program. Noticing and repairing unowned problems is a habit, not a bid for recognition.

artifactLines: Building things nobody requested, like his A/B testing frameworks, is a different trait from ambition.
The work he took on was explicitly not in scope and unassigned to him.
For three years in Haryana he operated inside the H.A.S.P. program.
Noticing and repairing unowned problems is a habit, not a bid for recognition.

beats.narration_text: The line that got me was that building A/B testing frameworks nobody asked for is different from ambition. I want to understand that distinction as a working habit, not a personality trait. Using his three years in Haryana within H.A.S.P. as the anchor, help me map it. Give me: (1) a definition separating out-of-scope initiative from ambition, (2) how to spot an unowned problem worth fixing, (3) two guardrails so fixing-what-nobody-asked doesn't derail my assigned work.

output: All of that is true and none of it is the point
That is a different thing from ambition
Sayam came to Northeastern having already run something
